title: Typed confirmation vs destructive action confirmation vs confirmation dialog vs password input
summary: Use typed confirmation when severe scope requires the user to reproduce an exact target phrase before commit; use destructive action confirmation for named destructive review without typing, confirmation dialog for other high-consequence choices, and password input for secrets used to authenticate.
decisionRules: Choose typed confirmation when one accidental activation could delete or reset a repository, project, workspace, account, production dataset, root credential, or broad permission scope and the user must prove they are targeting the exact object.

Choose destructive action confirmation when deletion needs object naming, loss inventory, safe cancellation, and dependency checks, but an exact typed phrase would add friction without improving target certainty.
Choose confirmation dialog when the decision is consequential but does not require target-text reproduction, such as exposing data, sending an irreversible message, or approving a cost.
Choose password input when users are entering a secret to authenticate or reauthenticate; typed confirmation text should be visible, non-secret, and compared against a known target phrase.

The required phrase should be the real object identifier, repository path, organization/project slug, or short destructive phrase shown in the same dialog; do not ask users to type vague words like yes for severe scope.
Disable the final destructive action until the normalized match rule passes, and show clear mismatch, exact-match, pasted, case-sensitive, and localization behavior.
Do not use typed confirmation for routine reversible cleanup, high-frequency list actions, or cases where users cannot reliably copy the required phrase.
Typed confirmation does not replace loss inventory, dependency blocking, permissions checks, audit logging, or a safe cancellation path.
failureModes: The required text is YES or DELETE, so users can pass the gate without verifying the affected object.
The system accepts a partial or wrong-case repository path even though the UI claims exact target verification.
The final destructive action enables before the user finishes typing or after stale text from another object remains in the field.
The field masks typed confirmation like a password, preventing users from checking the target phrase they typed.
The phrase is impossible to type on mobile or localized keyboards because it contains hidden whitespace, unsupported punctuation, or copy-only text.
The dialog relies on typed text but omits the loss inventory and dependency checks that would actually help the user decide.

sources.note: GitHub repository deletion requires warning acknowledgement and typing the repository name to verify the target.
GitLab project deletion requires entering the project name in the confirmation dialog before deleting.
Microsoft confirmation guidance supports specific consequence information and warns against unnecessary confirmation friction.
APG supplies alertdialog semantics for important modal decisions requiring a user response.
